content:
2 min read
1 hour ago

--

The better I became at reading code, the better I became at writing it.
When I first started programming, I believed that becoming a better developer meant writing more code.
More projects.
More exercises.
More features.
Every new line of code felt like progress.
But as I gained more experience, I realized something I hadn’t expected.
Writing code is only a small part of software development.
Understanding existing code is where most of the work actually happens.

My Perspective Changed
In the beginning, I spent almost all my time writing code.
Today, I spend a significant part of my day reading it.
Sometimes it’s code I wrote months ago.
Sometimes it’s documentation.
Sometimes it’s an open-source project.
Sometimes it’s a pull request from another developer.
The more I read, the more I realized that writing is only possible because someone else took the time to make their code understandable.

Reading Is Different from Writing
Writing code feels creative.
Reading code feels investigative.
When reading someone else’s work, you’re trying to answer questions like:

• What problem is this solving?

• Why was this approach chosen?

• What assumptions are hidden here?

• What business rule is being represented?

• What could break if this changes?
You’re not just reading syntax.
You’re reconstructing someone else’s reasoning.

The Question That Changed Everything
At first, I used to ask:
“What does this code do?”
Now I ask:
“Why was it written this way?”
That small change transformed the way I learn.
Instead of focusing only on the implementation, I started paying attention to the decisions behind it.
That’s where the real lessons are.
Reading Changed the Way I Write
Something interesting happened as I spent more time reading code.
I naturally started writing differently.
I began choosing clearer names.
Smaller methods.
More focused classes.
Not because I wanted my code to look cleaner.
But because I imagined someone reading it months later.
Very often, that someone is me.
Software Is a Conversation
One realization stood out to me.
Code isn’t a message for the compiler.
It’s a conversation with future developers.
Every variable name.
Every method.
Every class.
Every folder.
They all communicate intent.
If someone struggles to understand what I wrote, the problem isn’t always the reader.
Sometimes it’s the way I communicated the idea.
Reading Beyond Code
Over time, I realized that reading software isn’t limited to source code.
It’s also about reading:

• Documentation

• Architecture diagrams

• Pull Requests

• Commit history

• Issue discussions

• Technical decisions
All of these help explain why the software looks the way it does.
The code tells you what.
The surrounding context often tells you why.
A Habit I Didn’t Expect
Today, one of my favorite ways to learn is simply reading.
Not tutorials.
Not only books.
But real projects.
Open-source repositories.
Well-written documentation.
Older versions of my own code.
Each one exposes me to different ways of solving the same kind of problem.
That has become one of the fastest ways to improve my own thinking.
